summary: In his 1996 essay, Bill Gates predicts that content will be the primary driver of revenue on the Internet, much like the broadcasting industry. He argues that successful online content must be interactive and native to the medium, rather than just repurposed print. He also anticipates the rise of micro-transactions to fairly compensate creators and sustain the digital marketplace.

principles.statement: Content is where the real money will be made on the Internet.
principles.rationale: Just as in the television revolution, while hardware manufacturers spawn the industry, the long-term winners are those who use the medium to deliver information and entertainment.
principles.source_quote: Content is where I expect much of the real money will be made on the Internet, just as it was in broadcasting.

principles.statement: To be successful online, content must be deep, interactive, and native to the medium.
principles.rationale: Simply moving print content to a screen is insufficient to overcome the drawbacks of the medium. Users require up-to-date information, multimedia (audio/video), and opportunities for personal involvement.
principles.source_quote: But to be successful online, a magazine can’t just take what it has in print and move it to the electronic realm. There isn’t enough depth or interactivity in print content to overcome the drawbacks of the online medium.

principles.statement: For the Internet to thrive, content providers must be paid for their work.
principles.rationale: Without a viable way to generate revenue, interactive publishing remains merely a labor of love rather than a sustainable marketplace of ideas and products.
principles.source_quote: For the Internet to thrive, content providers must be paid for their work.

mental_models.name: The Internet as a Multimedia Photocopier
mental_models.description: Viewing the Internet as a tool that allows material to be duplicated at low cost and distributed worldwide at basically zero marginal cost, regardless of audience size.
mental_models.example: Anyone with a PC and a modem can publish whatever content they create, which challenges the future of venerable, high-priced printed scientific journals.

mental_models.name: The Broadcasting Analogy
mental_models.description: Comparing the Internet's evolution to the television revolution to predict where the long-term value will accrue.
mental_models.example: Recognizing that while manufacturing TV sets was a spawned industry, the long-term winners were the networks delivering information and entertainment.

heuristics: No company is too small to participate in supplying information or entertainment on the Internet.

signature_quotes.text: Content is where I expect much of the real money will be made on the Internet, just as it was in broadcasting.
In a sense, the Internet is the multimedia equivalent of the photocopier. It allows material to be duplicated at low cost, no matter the size of the audience.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the disruptive distribution power of the Internet.

signature_quotes.text: Those who succeed will propel the Internet forward as a marketplace of ideas, experiences, and products-a marketplace of content.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding thought on the future of the Internet.

anti_patterns.description: Taking what a publication has in print and simply moving it to the electronic realm.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: There isn't enough depth or interactivity in print content to overcome the drawbacks of reading on a computer screen. Users expect multimedia and personal involvement.

anti_patterns.description: Charging high subscription rates for an electronic community.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: As soon as a site charges a subscription, the number of visitors drops dramatically, which reduces the value proposition to advertisers.
